seoTitle: How to Create Epics and User Stories in Jira
metaDescription: Learn how to create epics and break them into user stories in Jira so large initiatives turn into actionable work.
intro: Turning a big idea into trackable work usually means starting with an epic and breaking it down into user stories your team can actually pick up. This guide covers creating both and connecting them together.

steps.heading: Create the epic first
steps.body: Open the create issue screen, select Epic, and give it a name that captures the overall initiative.

steps.heading: Add a description of the goal
steps.body: Write a short description explaining what the epic is meant to achieve so stories created under it stay aligned.

steps.heading: Break the epic into user stories
steps.body: Create individual stories, each describing a specific piece of user-facing functionality, and link them to the epic.

steps.heading: Write stories from the user's perspective
steps.body: Frame each story around what a user needs and why, rather than just a technical task description.

steps.heading: Prioritize and estimate the stories
steps.body: Order the stories by priority and add estimates so they're ready to move into sprint planning.

faq.question: How many user stories should one epic have?
faq.answer: There's no fixed number; it depends on the scope of the epic, though very large epics are usually broken into more manageable groups of stories.

faq.question: What's a good format for writing a user story?
faq.answer: A common format is 'As a [user], I want [goal], so that [reason]', which keeps the focus on user value.

faq.question: Can a story belong to more than one epic?
faq.answer: No, a story is typically linked to a single parent epic to keep the hierarchy clear.

faq.question: Should acceptance criteria be added to stories or epics?
faq.answer: Acceptance criteria are usually added at the story level since epics are broader and stories represent the testable unit of work.
